participants.reasoning: The extracted study describes 30 Korean adults with chronic low back pain recruited from a hospital and assigned to stabilization exercise vs conservative care, whereas the official study involved 60 Indian participants with chronic non-specific LBP recruited for a yoga intervention at an outpatient physiotherapy department and had different eligibility criteria.
participants.extracted: Thirty adults diagnosed with chronic low back pain by orthopedists at M Hospital in Daegu, Korea. The sample included 9 men and 21 women, with 15 assigned to a lumbar stabilization exercise group and 15 to a conservative treatment group. Mean age was 48.1 ± 6.9 years in the exercise group and 44.0 ± 6.7 years in the control group. Participants had no bone fractures, structural joint or nerve abnormalities, prior surgery, or infectious disease.
participants.official:
60 participants with chronic non‐specific LBP.
Settings: based at outpatient department of a physiotherapy college. Location of yoga intervention delivery appeared to be the outpatient department as no other settings were described.
Country: India.
Recruitment: self‐referral and healthcare practitioner referral through pamphlets and flyers.
Inclusion criteria: men and women aged ≥ 18 years who were ambulatory and had a history of non‐specific LBP persisting for ≥ 3 months.
Exclusion criteria: LB

interventions.reasoning: The extracted interventions describe a lumbar stabilization program versus conservative physical therapy over 6 weeks, which does not match the official yoga and individualized exercise interventions delivered over 4 weeks.
interventions.extracted: The intervention group performed a lumbar stabilization exercise program 3 times per week for 6 weeks. Each session lasted 40 minutes and included a 5-minute stretching warm-up, 30 minutes of lumbar stabilization exercises, and a 5-minute stretching cool-down. Exercises were performed while maintaining basal tonus by straining the transversus abdominis muscle and pelvic diaphragm and contracting the upper abdominal muscles for 10 seconds followed by 5 seconds of rest. The program included supine exercises with knees bent and arms at the sides, then alternating arm raises overhead, alternating leg raises from the ground, contralateral arm-and-leg raises, and hip raises held for 10 seconds; it also included crawling-position exercises such as alternating hand and leg lifts, contralateral arm-and-leg lifts, and hip backward and forward movements, each repeated 5 times. The comparison group received conservative physical therapy consisting of a hot pack for 20 minutes, interferential current therapy for 15 minutes, and ultrasound for 5 minutes per session, also 3 times per week for 6 weeks.
interventions.official:
Yoga group:(n = 30) weekly 60‐min iyengar yoga classes for 4 weeks. Used a series of 29 postures, including forward bends, twists, and inversions but excluding back bends. Poses progressed from simpler to more challenging over time. Used a range of props including sticks, mats, belts, blocks, chairs, wall ropes, benches, boxes, stools, trestle, and weights.
Home practice: requested 30 min, 5 days/week during intervention.

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes (ODI and lumbar lordosis angle at baseline and 6 weeks) do not match the official outcomes, which are pain and distress/limitations measured at 4 weeks and 6–7 months.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure functional disability using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), assessed as a percentage score derived from 10 items (0–5 each, summed and divided by 45 and converted to percent), at baseline and after 6 weeks of treatment. It also planned to measure lumbar lordosis angle (LLA) using plain radiography of the lumbar region with PACS-based angle measurement, at baseline and after 6 weeks of treatment.
outcomes.official:
Current pain (10‐cm VAS scale) at 4 weeks and 7 months.
Mean days of mental distress in previous 30 days at 4 weeks and 7 months.
Mean days of physical distress in previous 30 days at 4 weeks and 7 months.
